displayTitle: CBSE Class 6 Social Science - Chapter 3 Test 1

options.en: A physical feature on the surface of Earth

explanation.en: The chapter defines a landform as a physical feature on the surface of Earth.

questions.en: Which three broad categories of landforms are discussed in the chapter?

explanation.en: The chapter states that landforms can broadly be divided into mountains, plateaus and plains.

questions.en: Which journey in the chapter introduces three different landforms?

options.en: Chhota Nagpur to Prayagraj to Almora

explanation.en: The chapter asks students to imagine travelling from Chhota Nagpur to Prayagraj and then to Almora, encountering different landscapes.

options.en: Flat landforms below sea level only
Landforms much higher than the surrounding landscape

explanation.en: Mountains are landforms that are much higher than the surrounding landscape.

questions.en: Which features help us recognise mountains?

options.en: Broad base, steep slopes and narrow summit

explanation.en: The chapter says mountains can be recognised by a broad base, steep slopes and a narrow summit.

questions.en: What happens to snow on mountains at lower altitudes during summer?

options.en: It often melts and feeds rivers

explanation.en: At lower altitudes, mountain snow melts every summer and turns into water that feeds rivers.

options.en: The highest peaks of mountain ranges
Highlands with lower height, less steep slopes and rounded tops

explanation.en: The chapter describes hills as highlands lower than mountains, with less steep slopes and rounded tops.

options.en: Water from the atmosphere reaching the ground in any form

explanation.en: Precipitation means water from the atmosphere reaching the ground as rain, snow, hail and similar forms.

questions.en: Snow and hailstones are examples of water in which state?

explanation.en: The chapter explains that snow and hailstones are precipitation of water in a solid state.

questions.en: Most mountains of the world are grouped in what?

explanation.en: The chapter says most of the world’s mountains are grouped in mountain ranges.
